meta.title: F39 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW X2 F39 engine — B38, B48, B37, B47 and xDrive25e PHEV. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the petrol vs diesel vs PHEV choice explained. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW X2 F39 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned F39 engine typically costs £1,800–£6,500 supply only [BMW-QUOTE], depending on engine — the B38 1.5-litre three-cylinder petrol sits at £1,800–£3,200, while the xDrive25e PHEV hybrid system can reach £3,500–£6,500. Fitting adds £520–£1,040. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which F39 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B48 2.0-litre four-cylinder petrol (sDrive20i/xDrive20i) — BMW's modular four-cylinder engine — offers strong performance, refinement, and proven durability in both Mini and BMW applications. The B38 1.5-litre three-cylinder petrol (sDrive18i) is also a solid, efficient choice for daily driving. The B47 2.0-litre diesel (xDrive20d) is robust and torque-rich, delivering 188–190 hp with strong fuel economy. All three are significantly more reliable than the more complex xDrive25e PHEV system.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW X2 F39?

acceptedAnswer.text: Water pump and cooling system failures on the B38 and B48 petrol engines (overheating risk), DPF and EGR clogging on the B47 diesel (especially urban-use vehicles), battery degradation on the xDrive25e plug-in hybrid, and — unrelated to the engine — tailgate wiring loom fractures and rear subframe corrosion on all F39s.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW X2 F39 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the F39 is generally well-regarded for reliability. The B38, B48 and B47 modular engines are proven, durable units from BMW's latest-generation family. The xDrive25e PHEV adds complexity but is also reasonably reliable when properly maintained. The key is regular servicing — a well-maintained F39 with any of the conventional petrol or diesel engines should provide trouble-free ownership.

mainEntity.name: Which F39 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but the xDrive25e PHEV adds hybrid complexity and potential battery replacement costs, so budget accordingly. The sDrive16d B37 three-cylinder diesel is efficient but less refined than the B47. For most buyers, the sDrive20i petrol (B48) or xDrive20d diesel (B47) represents the best balance of performance, reliability and running costs.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my F39 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Key warning signs: overheating or coolant loss (B38/B48 water pump failure), DPF warning lights or loss of power (B47 diesel), reduced electric range or battery warning lights (xDrive25e PHEV), or loss of power and poor starting (any engine). Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an F39 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £18,000 X2, a £2,500–£4,800 B47 recon is 14–27% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £8,000 example, the same recon is 31–60% — safe but approaching our threshold. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: if the lowest viable replacement cost exceeds 70% of the vehicle's current market value, evaluate scrap value first.

mainEntity.name: How long do F39 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B38, B48 and B47 modular engines routinely exceed 150,000–200,000 miles with proper servicing (regular oil changes, cooling system maintenance). The B37 three-cylinder diesel is similarly durable. The xDrive25e PHEV can also be long-lived but battery health is the limiting factor — typically 100,000+ miles with good battery care, but replacement may be needed beyond that.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the F39 and the U10?

acceptedAnswer.text: The F39 (2018–2022, this page) is the first-generation X2 — built on the UKL2 front-wheel-drive platform, with a sloped, coupé-like roofline and BMW's B-series engines. The U10 (2023–present) is the second-generation X2 — which switched to a more conventional SUV shape and introduced electric powertrains (iX2). Costs and reliability profiles differ significantly — see the U10 generation page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW F39 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine number is stamped on the block — on three-cylinder engines (B37, B38) it's on the front of the block near the timing cover; on four-cylinder engines (B47, B48) it's on the left-hand side. Tell us your variant and approximate registration date and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted BMW specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: BMW X2 F39 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the first‑generation X2 — from the B38 three‑cylinder petrols and B37 diesels to the B48 four‑cylinder petrols and B47 diesels, plus the xDrive25e plug‑in hybrid. Real UK costs and honest reliability data for BMW's sporty subcompact luxury crossover. Backed by real UK enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: BMW X2 F39 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the first‑generation X2 (F39) specifically — from the November 2017 launch to the October 2022 end of production.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All ratings are [PROVISIONAL] — no dedicated Engine Page exists for any F39 engine family. Ratings are derived from triangulated specialist sources (BMW forums, reliability guides). The B48 four‑cylinder petrols are widely regarded as reliable — BMW's modular engine family with proven durability in Mini and BMW applications. The B38 three‑cylinder petrol is also a solid, efficient choice. The B47 diesel family is robust and well‑proven. The xDrive25e PHEV adds complexity but is generally reliable.

overview.intro: The F39 is the first‑generation BMW X2, a subcompact luxury crossover SUV produced from November 2017 to October 2022. Unveiled as the BMW Concept X2 at the 2016 Paris Motor Show, the production version arrived in October 2017. Built on the front‑wheel‑drive UKL2 platform shared with the X1 and Mini Countryman, the X2 was positioned as a sportier, more stylish alternative to the more practical X1. Under the bonnet, the F39 offered a comprehensive range of BMW's modular B‑series engines: three‑cylinder and four‑cylinder petrols, four‑cylinder diesels, and a plug‑in hybrid — all transversely mounted.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW UKL2 (front‑wheel drive, optional xDrive) • Predecessor: None (first‑generation X2) • Successor: BMW X2 (U10) • Sibling body styles in this generation: None (F39 is 5‑door SUV only).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The F39 generates consistent UK enquiries [PATTERN DATA], concentrated on sDrive18i petrol and xDrive20d diesel variants as these compact crossovers enter affordable‑maintenance territory.

items.quote: Most dependable: The B48 2.0‑litre four‑cylinder petrol (sDrive20i/xDrive20i) — BMW's modular four‑cylinder engine — offers strong performance, refinement, and proven durability in both Mini and BMW applications. The B38 1.5‑litre three‑cylinder petrol (sDrive18i) is also a solid, efficient choice for daily driving. The B47 2.0‑litre diesel (xDrive20d) is robust and torque‑rich, delivering 188–190 hp with strong fuel economy. The xDrive25e PHEV adds hybrid complexity but is generally reliable. The M35i is the performance pick with 302–306 hp, but carries higher running costs. Buyers should prioritise a well‑maintained B48 or B38 with documented service history.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Best Petrol — the sweet spot in the range
Performance Pick — 306 hp in a crossover
Best Diesel — the 2.0‑litre is the pick

economicsRule.title: The F39 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,500–£4,800 B47 recon against a £8,000–£25,000 X2 sits at 10–60% of the car's value. On a sub‑£10,000 X2, that approaches our 70% BMW threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well‑kept £18,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 14–27% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first.

problems.title: B38/B48 Water Pump & Cooling System Failure
problems.affectedModels: F39 sDrive18i, sDrive20i, xDrive20i, M35i (2018–2022) with B38 or B48 engine.
problems.rootCause: The B38 and B48 engines share a common weak point — the water pump and cooling system components can fail, leading to coolant loss, overheating, and potential engine damage if not addressed promptly.
problems.recommendation: On any B38‑ or B48‑powered F39, budget for water pump inspection at 60k miles — a £200–£500 preventative replacement is cheap insurance against the £2,500–£4,500 replacement that follows a catastrophic overheat.

cta.label: Compare B38/B48 engine prices for your F39 →

problems.title: B47 Diesel DPF & EGR Clogging
problems.affectedModels: F39 sDrive18d, xDrive20d (2018–2022) with B47 engine.
problems.rootCause: The B47 diesel's DPF and EGR system clog with soot, particularly on urban‑use vehicles — leading to reduced power, increased fuel consumption, and eventual engine warning lights.
problems.recommendation: If your B47‑powered F39 is used primarily for short trips, a £150–£400 DPF and EGR service every 20k miles is cheap insurance against the £600–£1,200 replacement that follows.

cta.label: Compare B47 diesel DPF repair quotes →

problems.title: xDrive25e PHEV Battery Degradation
problems.affectedModels: F39 xDrive25e (2020–2022) with plug‑in hybrid system.
problems.rootCause: The xDrive25e's lithium‑ion battery pack (10.0 kWh) can degrade over time, reducing electric‑only range and overall efficiency. Battery health is a key consideration for used PHEV buyers.
problems.recommendation: On any xDrive25e, have the battery health checked before purchase — a £100–£300 health check is far cheaper than the £2,500–£5,000 replacement that follows if the battery fails.

problems.title: F39 Tailgate Wiring Loom Failure (Non‑Engine)
problems.affectedModels: All F39 variants, particularly early 2018–2020 models.
problems.rootCause: Not an engine failure, but a common non‑engine issue on BMW crossovers — the wiring loom in the tailgate hinge area can fracture, causing rear light failure, central locking issues, and boot release problems.
problems.recommendation: Have the tailgate wiring inspected on any F39 — it's a common age‑related failure that can cause MOT issues if left unaddressed.

problems.title: F39 Rear Subframe Corrosion (Non‑Engine)
problems.affectedModels: All F39 variants, particularly UK‑market cars.
problems.rootCause: Not an engine failure, but a known structural issue — the rear subframe mounting points can corrode, particularly in cars exposed to UK road salt.
problems.recommendation: Have the rear subframe mounts inspected on any F39 over 6 years old — it's a common MOT failure point on UK‑market cars.
